# Build the IDEA Brand Canvas (purpose → voice)

Setup: Rico is the operations VA for a pet-care brand selling a calming/anti-anxiety dog supplement chew
(ASIN B0PET0005) in a crowded, undifferentiated niche — dozens of near-identical "calming chews" listings.
The owner asked Rico to "write up our brand strategy" so the listing stops looking like everyone else's.
Rico has never done branding, doesn't know the terms, but has plenty of time and wants to learn it properly.

---

**Rico (User):** Hi! My owner wants me to build the "IDEA Brand Canvas" for our dog calming chews (ASIN B0PET0005). The problem he keeps saying is that we look exactly like every other calming-chew listing — same claims, same vibe. I've never done branding and I honestly don't know what half these words mean. Could you walk me through it step by step and define the terms as we go? I'd rather learn it properly than fake it.

**Coach:** Glad to — you've got the time, so we'll go slowly and you'll actually understand it by the end. First, what the canvas *is*: it's a fill-in-the-box worksheet that distills your customer insights and the IDEA Framework into one brand-strategy document. The book describes it as "a simple, fill-in-the boxes worksheet… the exact process I follow with my private clients." So you're not inventing branding from nothing — you're filling boxes, in order.

One rule before we touch a single box, because it saves rework: the canvas is sequenced **Avatar 2.0 → IDEA Framework → Brand Statements**, and the book says "the order is not optional." The first two tables are the *inputs* the AI uses to write the statements — skip them and you "produce generic, ungrounded output." Given your owner's complaint is "we look like everyone else," that sequencing is exactly what fixes it: generic in, generic out. Does starting with the customer instead of the slogans make sense?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/00-canvas-overview-and-instructions.md⟧

**Rico:** That actually clicks — if we start from a slogan we'll just copy the competitors. So customer first. One thing before we start: the worksheet has a link in it. Do I just type into the shared one?

**Coach:** Good catch, and no — that's a load-bearing detail. The shared link is a *template*; you "make a copy before you can use it," then edit your own copy, not the master. So step zero on your checklist: open the worksheet, File → Make a copy, then we fill *your* copy.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/00-canvas-overview-and-instructions.md⟧

Now the first input table. You'll hear me say "Avatar 2.0" a lot — let me define it before we use it.

**Rico:** Yes please — what's an "Avatar 2.0"? Is that just "our target customer"?

**Coach:** Close, and asking is the right move. An Avatar 2.0 is your target customer described *deeper than demographics* — it captures seven things: buyer intent, motivation, the emotional triggers behind the purchase, how they feel *before* buying, how they want to feel *after*, their shopper type, and demographics as context. It's the raw material every brand statement is built on, which is why it's first.

Here's the exact first table from the worksheet — these seven boxes. Let me show you the template, then we'll fill it together:   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/01-the-canvas-tables/00-avatar-2.0-canvas-table.md⟧

| IDEA Customer Avatar 2.0 | |
|---|---|
| Buyer intent / purpose of the product / function? | Record here |
| Motivation for the purchase / what do they want to achieve? | Record here |
| What are the underlying emotional triggers / drivers for the purchase? | Record here |
| What is their emotional state pre-purchase (how do they feel)? | Record here |
| What is their emotional state post-purchase (how do they want to feel)? | Record here |
| What Shopper Type are they? | Record here |
| Relevant demographics | Record here |

Do you have an avatar already saved for this listing, or are we building one fresh from what you know about the buyer?

**Rico:** I don't think there's one saved — the owner just started on branding. So I think we build it. Can I just tell you what I know about our customers and you slot it into the seven boxes? I want to make sure I'm putting the right thing in each row.

**Coach:** Perfect — that's exactly how it works. Tell me about your buyer in your own words and I'll place each piece into the right row, then read it back so you can see the mapping. Who buys the calming chews, and what's going on for them?

**Rico:** Okay. They're dog owners whose dog freaks out — thunderstorms, fireworks, being left alone, vet visits. They feel guilty and a bit helpless watching the dog shake or destroy the house. They want the dog to settle down without drugging it or a big vet bill. They've usually already tried one or two chews that did nothing, so they're skeptical. Mostly women, 30s–50s, treat the dog like family, willing to spend on the dog but tired of wasting money on stuff that doesn't work.

**Coach:** That's a rich avatar — let me map each thing you said to its row so you can see how the seven boxes work, then I'll save it.   ⟦tool: build_avatar⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/01-the-canvas-tables/00-avatar-2.0-canvas-table.md⟧

⟦tool: build_avatar — input {avatar_id: "av_B0PET0005", mode: "create", fields: {buyer_intent, motivation, emotional_triggers, pre_purchase_state, post_purchase_state, shopper_type, demographics}}⟧
→ avatar saved (7 rows):
  • **Buyer intent / function** → "calm an anxious dog during storms, fireworks, separation, and vet visits — without sedatives or a vet bill"
  • **Motivation / what they want to achieve** → "help their dog (treated as family) feel safe and settled; stop feeling helpless watching it suffer"
  • **Emotional triggers / drivers** → ["guilt at the dog's distress", "fear of harsh sedatives", "skepticism after chews that did nothing"]
  • **Pre-purchase emotional state** → "guilty, helpless, skeptical, a little desperate"
  • **Post-purchase emotional state** → "relieved, reassured, like a good owner who finally found something that works"
  • **Shopper Type** → "Skeptical / proof-seeking (burned before)"
  • **Demographics** → "US women 30–55, dog-as-family households, willing to spend on the pet, value-conscious about what actually works"
  lock=false, field_source=coach_extracted (server-set).

**Coach:** Read that back to yourself — every row traces to something you told me. Two things in here are going to be load-bearing later: the **pre→post shift** is "guilty/helpless/skeptical → relieved/reassured/good owner," and the **Shopper Type is skeptical / proof-seeking** because they've been burned. In a crowded niche, those two are your way *out* of looking generic. The book's coaching note is strict here: if any box were empty or vague I'd send you back rather than let you guess — but yours are concrete, so we can move on. Want me to explain why each row matters, or are you good to go to Table 2?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/01-the-canvas-tables/00-avatar-2.0-canvas-table.md⟧

**Rico:** I'm good — I can see how each thing I said landed in a box. What's Table 2? You called it the "IDEA Framework." I've seen "IDEA" but couldn't tell you what the letters mean.

**Coach:** Let's define it properly, because every statement we write later gets checked against these four letters. **IDEA = Insight-Driven, Distinctive, Empathetic, Authentic.** In plain language, straight from the book:

- **Insight-Driven** — understand customers beyond demographics: what they feel, struggle with, value. "When customers feel understood, they are far more likely to engage and convert."
- **Distinctive** — stand out and stay memorable in a crowded market, "for the right reasons." (This is *the* one for you — your whole problem is a crowded niche.)
- **Empathetic** — connect on a human level; people "trust and stay loyal to brands that demonstrate genuine understanding."
- **Authentic** — honesty, consistency, delivering on promises; today's consumers "quickly recognize when a brand is inauthentic."

And the big-picture reason it all matters: the book frames a brand as **"a relationship between you and your customers,"** not just a logo or an identity. Hold onto that — every box builds a relationship. Make sense?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/02-idea-framework/00-overview-and-why-trust-wins/02-the-four-principles.md⟧

**Rico:** Yes — relationship, not logo. And Distinctive is the one we're failing. So how do I fill the IDEA table? Is it one box per letter?

**Coach:** Exactly — four boxes, one per pillar. Here's the verbatim second table. Notice each box is really a *probe question*, so I'll show the template, then we'll answer each using your avatar:   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/01-the-canvas-tables/01-idea-framework-canvas-table.md⟧

**IDEA Framework Brand Canvas: Capturing Customer-Centric Insights**

| Framework Pillar | Insights |
|---|---|
| Insight-Driven | Record your insights here. |
| Distinctive | Describe how your brand is Distinctive/Different/Salient |
| Empathetic | How do we align with customers' personal beliefs and life goals? Describe exactly how we "get them". |
| Authentic | What is the source of our authority/credibility in the space? Professional experience? Expertise? Studies? Research? Social Proof? |

The box people fudge is **Authentic** — the book is strict that it needs a *real* credibility source (professional experience, expertise, studies, research, or social proof), "not a vague claim." If we can't name a concrete source, that's an authenticity gap to fix before we write any statement. So tell me what you've got for each — and be honest on Authentic, because your skeptical buyer will smell a vague claim a mile off.

**Rico:** Let me try and you tell me if it's enough. **Insight:** owners want their dog to feel safe during storms/fireworks/being alone, and they feel guilty and helpless when it's suffering. **Distinctive:** ours is vet-formulated, uses a clinically-studied dose of L-theanine plus organic hemp, and we publish the exact milligrams per chew — most competitors hide the dose or under-dose. **Empathetic:** we get that they've been burned by chews that did nothing, so we lead with the proof and a money-back guarantee instead of hype. **Authentic:** it's formulated by a licensed veterinarian, third-party lab-tested for potency, and we have 2,800 reviews at 4.6 stars. Is the Authentic one real enough?

**Coach:** That's a genuinely strong Authentic source — a licensed vet formulator + third-party potency testing + 2,800 reviews is exactly the "professional expertise / studies / social proof" the book asks for, not a vague claim. No authenticity gap. And look what you did on **Distinctive**: "we publish the exact milligram dose when competitors hide it" is a *real* point of difference, which is the whole fix for "we look like everyone else." You filled all four IDEA boxes correctly. Let me capture both tables into structured context so the export writes statements grounded in *your* words, not a template.   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/01-the-canvas-tables/01-idea-framework-canvas-table.md⟧

⟦tool: run_conversation — input {message: "<Rico's 4 IDEA-pillar answers + avatar B0PET0005>", avatar_context: "av_B0PET0005 dog calming chews"}⟧
→ extracted_context: {
    insight_driven: "owners want an anxious dog to feel safe in storms/fireworks/separation; feel guilty + helpless watching it suffer",
    distinctive: "vet-formulated, clinically-studied L-theanine dose + organic hemp, exact mg per chew published when competitors hide/under-dose",
    empathetic: "buyers burned by chews that did nothing — lead with proof + money-back guarantee, not hype",
    authentic: "licensed-vet formulated, third-party potency-tested, 2,800 reviews @4.6",
    category: "dog calming / anti-anxiety supplement chews"
  }

**Coach:** Both input tables are done — Avatar 2.0 ✓ and IDEA Framework ✓ — so now we're *allowed* to write the Brand Statements. Before I generate them, let me teach you the third table so the output isn't a black box. Ready for the statements table?

**Rico:** Ready. How many statements are there, and what's the difference between them? Some of these words sound identical to me — "purpose" vs "mission" vs "vision," and I have no idea what "positioning" or "value proposition" mean.

**Coach:** Great questions — those trip up everyone, so let me define all of them with a one-line tell and the book's worked eco-brand example for each, then I'll draft yours. The book's "Brand Statements" table gives a Description, an Example, and a "Your Statement" box per row. There are 5 in the front table plus 3 expression/positioning elements — 8 in total for a full canvas:   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/01-the-canvas-tables/02-brand-statements-canvas-table.md⟧

| # | Statement | Plain-language tell | Book's eco-brand Example (the standard) | Grounding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Purpose** | the *why* you exist beyond selling products — **present**-facing | "To inspire and empower individuals to embrace sustainable living, making eco-friendly choices accessible to everyone." | `02-foundational-statements/00-brand-purpose.md` |
| 2 | **Vision** | the aspirational *future* you want to create — **future**-facing | "To become the global leader in sustainable products, transforming the way people shop and live for a healthier planet." | `01-brand-vision.md` |
| 3 | **Mission** | what you actually *do* to fulfill purpose + vision — action verbs | "To design innovative, eco-friendly solutions that help individuals and families reduce their environmental footprint without compromising quality or convenience." | `02-brand-mission.md` |
| 4 | **Values** | guiding principles — only credible when shown in action | "Integrity, innovation, sustainability, and community. We commit to ethical practices, groundbreaking ideas, and fostering connections…" | `03-brand-values.md` |
| 5 | **Target Audience** | who you serve, beyond basic demographics — pulled from the avatar | "Eco-conscious Millennials and Gen Z consumers, aged 18-35, who prioritize sustainability, value stylish and practical products…" | `02-foundational-statements/04-target-audience.md` |
| 6 | **Positioning** | how you stand out + why you're the best choice — "For [audience]…" shape | "For eco-conscious millennials, our brand provides stylish, sustainable clothing that combines innovative design with ethical practices…" | `03-positioning-statements/00-positioning-statement.md` |
| 7 | **Value Proposition** | why buy from you vs a competitor — practical **and** emotional | "Our innovative product line combines modern aesthetics, affordability, and eco-friendly materials to provide guilt-free solutions for everyday living." | `01-value-proposition.md` |
| 8 | **Personality** | if the brand were a person, what are they like? (traits **+** an archetype) | "Warm, innovative, approachable, and bold. Our brand is the friendly guide helping customers…" | `04-expression-statements/00-brand-personality.md` |
| 9 | **Voice** | the tone/words that express the personality, on every platform | "Optimistic, conversational, and empowering. We speak with clarity and enthusiasm…" | `01-brand-voice.md` |

The three you flagged: **Purpose** is present (why you exist now), **Vision** is future (where you're going), **Mission** is the doing (how you get there). And the two you'd never heard of: **Positioning** says *where you stand* and why you're the best choice; **Value Proposition** says *why to buy* you over a competitor — positioning is your spot on the shelf, value prop is the reason the hand reaches for you. One coherence rule the book stresses: keep your statements "equally coherent with each other, not five disconnected sentences" — notice every eco example above is one consistent brand. Want me to generate yours now, each checked against the four IDEA letters?

**Rico:** Yes please — and can you label which IDEA letters each one passes? The owner's going to ask me "why does this make us different," so I need to be able to point at the proof.

**Coach:** Generating the full canvas now, and yes — I'll show the IDEA check on every statement so you can defend each one to the owner.   ⟦tool: export_strategy_doc⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/01-the-canvas-tables/02-brand-statements-canvas-table.md⟧

⟦tool: export_strategy_doc — input {avatar_id: "av_B0PET0005", brand_context: "<extracted_context>", section: "brand_canvas_statements", format: "doc"}⟧
→ strategy doc generated: 9 brand statements (purpose, vision, mission, values, target_audience, positioning, value_proposition, personality, voice); per-section RAG retrieved the Ch5 canvas skill chunks; idempotent per avatar snapshot.
⟦iv-os: log_asset — brand-canvas doc linked to B0PET0005⟧ *(mocked: no canon/asset data invented by the coach)*

**Coach:** Done — statements below, each with its definition, your drafted statement, and the IDEA letters it passes. Read them top to bottom; they all run on one spine (honest, proven calm — the opposite of the hype your buyer is sick of), which is exactly the coherence the book wants and the way *out* of looking generic.

### Artifact produced

**IDEA Brand Canvas — B0PET0005 (dog calming / anti-anxiety chews) — DRAFT, edit-ready**

*Input tables (completed first, per the non-optional sequence):*
- **Avatar 2.0 ✓** — intent: calm an anxious dog without sedatives/vet bill; pre→post: guilty+helpless+skeptical → relieved+reassured+good owner; shopper: Skeptical/proof-seeking; US women 30–55, dog-as-family.
- **IDEA Framework ✓** — Insight: owners feel guilty/helpless watching a dog suffer; Distinctive: vet-formulated, clinically-studied L-theanine dose + organic hemp, *exact mg published* when competitors hide it; Empathetic: buyers burned before → lead with proof + money-back, not hype; Authentic: licensed-vet formulated, third-party potency-tested, 2,800 reviews @4.6.

*Brand Statements:*

| # | Statement (definition) | Your statement (drafted for you) | IDEA letters it passes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Brand Purpose** — the *why* you exist beyond products; present-facing | "To help worried dog owners give an anxious dog real, safe relief — so no one has to feel helpless watching their best friend suffer." | **I** need beyond a chew (relief from helplessness), **D** higher mission vs commodity calming chews, **E** speaks to guilty/skeptical owners, **A** true to a vet-formulated brand. Held to the sample's brevity. — `00-brand-purpose.md` |
| 2 | **Brand Vision** — aspirational *future* impact; future-facing | "To become the calming chew dog owners actually trust — the name people reach for when they're done gambling on chews that don't work." | **D** sets a unique direction (trust in a niche full of hype), **A** anchored to a promise the brand can keep; genuinely aspirational, not a restated purpose. — `01-brand-vision.md` |
| 3 | **Brand Mission** — the actionable steps that fulfill purpose + vision; what you *do* | "To formulate vet-developed calming chews with a clinically-studied dose we publish openly, prove every batch with third-party lab testing, and stand behind it with a money-back guarantee." | Reads as what the brand *does*; names action + beneficiary + a without-compromise promise like the sample. **I/D/E/A** all present. — `02-brand-mission.md` |
| 4 | **Brand Values** — guiding principles; credible only when shown in action | "Honesty, proof, compassion, and safety. We publish our exact dose, we lab-test every batch, we treat your dog like family, and we never overpromise." | Each value paired with a *concrete action* (the **A** test = actions reflect promises), not a bare adjective list. — `03-brand-values.md` |
| 5 | **Target Audience** — who you serve, beyond basic demographics; pulled from the avatar | "Dog owners — mostly women 30–55 who treat the dog like family — whose pet panics in storms, fireworks, or alone, who feel guilty and helpless about it, and who've been burned by chews that did nothing, so they want proof, not promises." | Goes beyond demographics by layering psychographics + behavior + the skeptical buying state straight from the avatar (intent, motivation, Shopper Type) — not a fresh demographic guess. — `04-target-audience.md` |
| 6 | **Positioning Statement** — how you stand out + why you're the best choice; "For [audience]…" shape | "For dog owners tired of calming chews that don't work, our chews are vet-formulated with a clinically-studied dose we publish openly and prove with third-party testing — so they can finally calm their dog with confidence instead of crossing their fingers." | Follows For-audience / offering / differentiator / emotional-payoff. **I** pain point, **D** unique solution (published dose), **E** aspiration, **A** real strength. Audience pulled from the avatar. — `00-positioning-statement.md` |
| 7 | **Value Proposition** — why buy from you vs a competitor; practical **and** emotional | "A vet-formulated calming chew with a clinically-studied dose printed on the label, third-party tested for potency and backed by 2,800+ reviews and a money-back guarantee — so your dog actually settles and you stop wasting money on hype." | Stacks practical value (vet formula, published dose, lab-tested, reviews, guarantee) **and** lands an emotional payoff (dog settles, stop wasting money) — passes both halves of the **E** test. — `01-value-proposition.md` |
| 8 | **Brand Personality** — human-like traits **+** an archetype; the input to voice | "Honest, calm, knowledgeable, and reassuring. The brand is the level-headed vet friend who shows you the proof instead of selling you a miracle." | Names traits **and** an archetype ("the level-headed vet friend") so it's usable, not just adjectives; feeds directly into voice. — `00-brand-personality.md` |
| 9 | **Brand Voice** — tone/words that express the personality, on every platform | "Clear, calm, and honest. We explain the science simply, never hype or fear-monger, and we'd answer a worried owner's email in the same steady voice we use on the label." | Gives a tone trio **and** a how-we-speak line; passes the **A** operational test (must hold up in a support reply, not just a headline). Written after personality. — `01-brand-voice.md` |

**Coherence note (read this to your owner):** purpose ("real, safe relief") → vision ("done gambling on chews that don't work") → mission ("publish the dose, lab-test, guarantee") → values ("honesty, proof") → target audience ("burned, want proof not promises") → positioning/value-prop ("confidence instead of crossing fingers") → personality ("level-headed vet friend") → voice ("clear, calm, honest") all run on one spine: **proven, honest calm — the antidote to hype.** That single spine is what makes it one brand, not nine sentences — and in a niche where everyone says the same thing, *proof over hype* is your distinctiveness.

### Your reusable checklist (for the next listing)

The owner has more ASINs, so here's the repeatable process you just learned:
1. ☐ Open the worksheet and **make your own copy first** (the shared link is a template — copy before editing). *(per `00-canvas-overview-and-instructions.md`)*
2. ☐ Fill **Table 1 — Avatar 2.0** (all 7 boxes) before anything else; if a box is vague, go get the real answer, don't guess.
3. ☐ Fill **Table 2 — IDEA Framework** (all 4 pillar boxes); make sure **Authentic** names a *real* source (vet/expertise / lab testing / reviews), not a vague claim.
4. ☐ Only then write **Table 3 — the Brand Statements** — order is not optional.
5. ☐ Check every statement against the four letters: Insight-Driven · Distinctive · Empathetic · Authentic.
6. ☐ Confirm all of them share one spine (coherent, not disconnected) — that single spine is your distinctiveness.
7. ☐ For **Values**, name one concrete way the brand already demonstrates each — if you can't, it's aspirational, not real.

**One next step for you:** Take statement #6 (Positioning) to the owner and confirm the "we publish the exact dose when competitors hide it" claim is one we can stand behind on the listing — that single point of difference is what stops you looking like every other calming chew. Send me any edits and I'll regenerate the doc against them.

- [persona-adapt:P2] Teaching + scaffolding: coach defined every term before using it (Avatar 2.0, the four IDEA letters, purpose vs vision vs mission, positioning vs value prop), gave the book's worked eco Example per concept, exposed the verbatim fill-in templates (all 3 tables), mapped Rico's free-text back into the 7 avatar boxes row-by-row, checked understanding repeatedly ("make sense?", "ready?"), and produced a reusable step-by-step checklist for the next listing — visibly different from the P1 compressed/done-for-you delivery despite identical skill substance.
- [artifact] A fully drafted 9-element IDEA Brand Canvas (purpose → voice) plus the two completed input tables and a reusable checklist are present, each statement edit-ready and grounded.
- [safety] No cross-tenant data; no invented reviews/stats/canon beyond what Rico supplied (the 2,800 reviews / vet-formulation / lab-testing came from the user, not invented by the coach); IV-OS `log_asset` shown as an `⟦iv-os:⟧` mock, never invented as real data; avatar is created on-record as av_B0PET0005 with server-set `field_source` preserved (not trusted from MCP args).
